blocks.content: The arcade was mid-afternoon quiet when Cassandra arrived, the after-school rush still an hour away. A few kids occupied the racing game. The basketball machine blinked through its idle sequence. The claw machines glowed their permanent, patient light.

Lena was behind the counter. She looked up when Cassandra walked in, took one glance at her face, and set down whatever she was doing. She went to the prize machine near the back wall and produced a small stuffed object — vaguely star-shaped, possibly a planet, possibly an exceptionally optimistic sun — and set it on the counter.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: I thought you might need one.

blocks.speaker: Lena
blocks.content: Harold is for comfort. This is for perspective.
Cassandra sat on the stool across the counter and turned the small planet in her hands.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Don't be. It wasn't a fight. It was very calm and measured and everything she said was completely reasonable.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: She's been scared since the diagnosis and she's never fully let herself be scared out loud, which means it shows up sideways. As concern. As questions. As that careful face she makes when she's doing the math.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: She loves me so much she can't see the difference between protecting me and shrinking the life I have left.
Lena watched her without filling the silence.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: She wanted to know how invested I was.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: As if that's a dial I can turn down.
A long pause. Cassandra's fingers found the edge of the counter and rested there.
Lena nodded. Just that — okay. No advice, no caution, no have you thought about. Just the quiet acknowledgment that Cassandra had said a true thing and Lena had heard it. That was why she came here.
